learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate skills of data collection based on observation and note-taking.
Demonstrate understanding of theoretical frameworks for analysing intercultural experience and competence
Engage in reflective processes for understanding and evaluating intercultural experiences and challenges
Apply problem-solving and critical thinking skills in intercultural contexts
Relate international experience to theoretical frameworks
Articulate and communicate intercultural understanding effectively in a variety of genres

In order to accommodate different academic calendars at our overseas Exchange partner institutions, there are six opportunities to submit a fieldnote: you only need to submit five.
assessments.task_description:
This assessment item encourages you to approach your in-country study as fieldwork, compiling a shared database of fieldnotes which will be analyzed in Semester 1 2027. In order to achieve the dual goals of building a shared database and undertaking assessment, each fieldnote should be submitted twice:

  • to the shared database on a padlet in the folder corresponding to this assessment item on the course website.
  • to Turnitin (via a link on the course website) in order to receive personal, private, feedback.

Please remember to submit the work twice - even if you make the deadline with one submission, the work will not be marked until both copies have been uploaded.

What is a fieldnote? Your fieldnote uses a template provided on the course website to record the important details of a precise observation you have personally made during your Exchange Abroad experience. Note: "your Exchange Abroad experience" includes during travel undertaken immediately before, during or immediately after your semester abroad. Experiences of later travel, or of experiences at home are not acceptable. You explain why this observation is interesting as data relating to your chosen theme (see below), and note any questions it raises for you. Your focus is on the observation and questions it raises, which might themselves be answered by further observations. You can relate your observation, briefly, to wider patterns of behaviour, but your aim is not to describe those wider patterns. You may include photographs or recordings. The shared padlet proposes four initial themes for these observations, students can propose an additional theme for consideration. You do not need to post on all the themes. Note: you can post additional observations to the padlet, above 5 in total: simply upload to Turnitin those you wish to be marked. 

The main fieldnote padlet contains links to the four thematic padlets, corresponding to the themes:

  • Cultural practices (doing things differently)
  • Intercultural practices (practices of mediation; negotiation, accommodation, successful or otherwise).
  • Embodiment (being there; contact through the senses)
  • Linguistic landscapes (language use in the host culture; language use amongst exchange students; language learning)

Each entry will be at least 150 words. See the course website for further details of the information to include in each entry.

How many fieldnotes are required and when should they be posted ? Five fieldnotes will contribute to your mark, but there are six opportunities to submit, to cater for different starting dates. You submit by the last working day of each month: August, September, October, November, December, January. Remember, nothing stops you submitting a fieldnote early, or submitting a fieldnote when you are no longer in-country as long as it relates to an observation you made during Study Abroad.

Marking criteria: Each field note is marked out of 5% on the quality of: Information provided; Comments; Language. (See detailed grid on the course website)

Use of Generative AI and Machine Translation: A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Each entry must be submitted via Turnitin AND posted on the shared padlet. Links to both are available via the course website “Assessment” section. If one part has been submitted on time, then the work is considered to have been submitted by the deadline, but the fieldnote will not be marked until it has been uploaded correctly, both as a piece of assessment (on Turnitin) and as a contribution to the shared archive (on the appropriate Padlet).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Report on tutorial discussion of a reading
assessments.due_date:
19/03/2027 - 7/06/2027

In Sem 1, 2027, students will write up discussion of one reading, based on a set article and work in class in their choice of: Week 3, Week 5, Week 9, Week 11 or Week 13.

The report is due at the end of the week following the tutorial, i.e. by the end of Week 4, Week 6, Week 10, Week 12, or, in the case of a report on Week 13, by the Monday of exam period.
assessments.task_description:
Once during the semester following your Study Abroad experience, you write a report on class discussion of one of the set readings: you summarise the paper, class discussion of it, and evaluate its usefulness in terms of analyzing the fieldnotes submitted by your cohort. This evaluation of usefulness should include an example of how the article could be applied to a specific fieldnote (or a cogent argument as to how it cannot be applied to our data set and the kind of data which would be needed to use the theoretical framework outlined in the reading). This assessment item provides you with training for writing your final analytical essay.

You sign up ahead of time to choose the particular reading for which you will be responsible and so you will be able to do some preparation of your report before the actual tutorial discussion.

Fuller guidelines will be posted on the course website.

Length: 1000 words, not including references

  • If your paper is under 900 words, your mark will be reduced proportionally. For example, if your initial mark is 14/20, but you only wrote 500 words, rather than 1000, your marks is reduced to 7/20.
  • If your paper is over 1 100 words, the excess material may not be read. For example, if by the 1 100 word point, you are still explaining the methodology used by the authors of the article and haven't yet got to your evaluation of the usefulness of the article you won't get points for that evaluation.

Marking criteria: the report will be marked on the quality of the summary, of the evaluation of the applicability of the article to your fieldnotes (including the example) and of the written expression. (A detailed rubric is provided on the course website).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Pre-departure Seminar for Future Study Abroad Participants
assessments.due_date:
12/04/2027 - 16/04/2027

WEEK 7

The briefing session takes place in the usual class in Week 7, with other times scheduled if student numbers require it. The timeslot to which students assign themselves will be taken as the due date for the assessment item; or, if a student does not sign up, the last scheduled presentation session will be considered to be the due date.
assessments.task_description:
Based on their own Exchange Abroad experience (and, potentially, that of their peers), COMU3065 students present a short talk providing precise, useful advice for students interested in undertaking Exchange Abroad in the future. The presentation is given to a live audience consisting of any interested students, and peers enrolled in COMU3065, and is also recorded for students unable to attend. Note: in the past, only small numbers of students not enrolled in COMU3065 have attended the live sessions, but they are your core audience. If you do not want your presentation recorded and made available to students, please email the course co-ordinator.

You should pick a specific topic of relevance to your audience, which you should announce in the introduction to your presentation. For example, you might cover topics such as:

  1. Negotiating the academic system in [host country]. What did you learn from negotiating the differences? Were there challenges and how did you overcome them? How could others learn from your experience?
  2. Intercultural communication in [host country]. What advice would you give to students interested in the country where you did your exchange? Share your intercultural insights!
  3. Developing your skillset through Study Abroad. What skills and attributes have you developed during Study Abroad which have contributed to your personal and professional development, and how could other students benefit in the same way (or learn from your errors).
  4. Self-care and Study Abroad. Based on your experience, what advice can you give your audience members so that they cope and thrive in the sometimes challenging Study Abroad environment?

Please note: if you are also providing a text to the Global Experiences team, or to your scholarship sponsor, you can draw on that work for this presentation if the content is suitable for the task requirements: however, this is a presentation, not the reading of a script.

Length and format: This presentation should be 6-8 minutes long, be supported by a visual aid (e.g. Powerpoint, Canva) and be followed by responding to 3 to 4 questions from the audience. Internal students present in-person, external students present on Zoom.

Marking criteria: This assessment item will be marked on the quality of: the information provided and its relationship to your experience; presentation skills; language. (For a detailed rubric, see the course website.)

Permitted materials:

Preparation: Students may use any resources, including Generative AI and Machine Translation, to prepare their presentation.

Presentation itself: Students may use the support of their visual aid (Powerpoint/ Canva etc) and notes on paper, if necessary. However, no other resources should be used in the presentation.
assessments.submission_guidelines: In person or on Zoom (for external students only).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students write an essay which draws on a selection of the fieldnotes, relates them to theoretical frameworks presented in class for analysing intercultural experiences, competencies and study abroad, to provide a precise analysis leading to a logical conclusion. Students are invited to discuss their plan for their essay with the course co-ordinator before starting to write it. The essay should be clearly structured with

  • a title
  • an introduction stating the precise topic to be discussed
  • a summary of relevant literature
  • a brief indication of the method by which the field notes and examples were selected
  • a discussion section
  • a conclusion. 

(More detailed guidelines are provided on the course website along with the essay questions.)

What to include:

  • Precise references to examples in the Field Notes. 
  • References to the readings studied throughout semester. 
  • References to concepts discussed in class.

Length: 2000 words (1 800 - 2 200 words, excluding the reference list and any appendices). Below 1 800, and the mark will be reduced proportionally. For example 30/35 for an essay which is 1500 words long will be reduced to 1500/2000 x 30. Markers will not read beyond 2 200 words.

Marking criteria: The essay will be marked on the quality of: the demonstrated understanding of theory discussed during the course; the analysis; organisation of the essay and its argument; academic writing. A detailed marking rubric is available on the course Blackboard site.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Essays should be uploaded to the Turnitin Link on the course website.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Semester Two 2026: Study Abroad

Your Study Abroad experience overlaps with UQ's semester two 2024. You may start and finish earlier or later, but you are participating in your Study Abroad experience. During this time, your contribution to COMU3065 takes the form of completing five fieldnotes - see the assessment section for details.


Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L06
